headline: The 2022 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Aaron Judge Fell to Pick 22 and Everyone Pretended That Was Normal
subheadline: A retrospective autopsy of 14 teams making 210 decisions, some brilliant, some baffling, and one that looked like someone's cat walked across the keyboard during Round 4.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane throws a chair across the room because his scouts won't stop talking about a prospect's girlfriend. I think about that scene every March when fantasy baseball drafts begin, because somewhere in America, a grown adult is yelling at their spouse about whether Lance Lynn is a Round 4 value (spoiler: he was not, and we'll get there). The 2022 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft took place on March 22nd, roughly 72 hours before Opening Day, which meant everyone had just enough spring training data to feel confident and not nearly enough to actually BE confident. The perfect recipe for chaos.

This was a 14-team snake draft with 12 categories — the kind of scoring system that separates the casual fantasy dabbler from the person who has a spreadsheet tab dedicated to WHIP projections by ballpark. We had dynasty hopefuls, we had teams coming off top-four finishes looking to reload, and we had at least three managers whose draft strategies appeared to be "close my eyes and click." The big storylines? **Aaron Judge** somehow sliding to pick 22 (before he hit 62 home runs, but still), **Fernando Tatis Jr.** getting selected in Round 9 like some kind of charity case (which, uh, turned out to be prescient in the worst possible way), and someone taking **Nelson Cruz** in Round 10 like it was still 2019. This draft had everything: vindication, heartbreak, and at least two picks that made me audibly say "wait, what?" while reviewing the data.

The best part about retrospective draft reviews is that we get to be the annoying guy at the poker table who says "I would have folded that" after seeing the river card. We KNOW that **Paul Goldschmidt** won the MVP. We KNOW that **Shane McClanahan** became an ace. We know that **Walker Buehler** threw his last healthy pitch sometime around June. And now, armed with that information, we get to judge 14 fantasy managers like they should have seen it all coming. It's deeply unfair. It's wildly entertaining. Let's get into it.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

**BaseballPro77** held the first overall pick — the pole position, the dream slot, the "you literally cannot mess this up" spot — and went with **Trea Turner**. Not Juan Soto. Not Ronald Acuña Jr. (who was coming off an ACL tear, so fair). Trea Turner. And you know what? Turner hit .298 with 21 homers and 27 steals. That's not embarrassing. But when you consider that Soto went 3rd and Jose Ramírez went 4th... we'll call it "defensible but not exciting." It's like ordering chicken fingers at a steakhouse. Nobody's going to yell at you, but you're not getting invited back to the steakhouse.

The real first-round drama was at the backend. **General Hospital** had picks 14 AND 15 thanks to the snake, and they walked away with **Ronald Acuña Jr.** and **Rafael Devers**. That's either the steal of the draft or a catastrophe waiting to happen, depending entirely on Acuña's knee. (Spoiler: it was kind of both.) Meanwhile, **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** took **Gerrit Cole** at 13 — a starting pitcher in the first round of a 12-category league where you need counting stats from hitters. Bold strategy, Cotton.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**BaseballPro77 (Pick #1)**
Coming off a 4th-place finish in 2021, BaseballPro77 had aspirations of a championship run. They opened with **Trea Turner**, followed by **Matt Olson** (solid, if unspectacular — .240 with 34 HR) and **Starling Marte** (who gave them .292 and 18 steals before injuries derailed him). The pitching strategy was interesting: **Lance Lynn** in Round 4 turned into a disaster when he threw just 72 innings with a 4.24 ERA, but **Kevin Gausman** (Round 5, 2.81 ERA) and **Carlos Rodón** (Round 6, 2.88 ERA, 237 Ks) were legitimate aces. The **Framber Valdez** pick at 113 was sneaky genius — 201 IP, 2.82 ERA. But then there's **Nelson Cruz** in Round 10, who hit .234 with 10 home runs like a man who should have retired two years earlier. This is a B-minus draft that could have been a B-plus with literally any other Round 10 pick.
**Grade: B-**

**Zach's Team (Pick #2)**
After finishing 9th in 2021, Zach needed a bounce-back draft. He got **Vladimir Guerrero Jr.** at 2 overall, which looked genius after Vladdy's 2021 but turned into a slight disappointment in '22 (.274, 32 HR — good, not MVP-level). **Lucas Giolito** in Round 2 was a disaster waiting to happen (4.90 ERA, complete implosion). But here's where it gets interesting: **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 6 gave elite production before injury (.254, 14 HR, 12 SB in 60 games), and **Emmanuel Clase** in Round 7 was dominant in the NSVH categories (42 saves, 1.36 ERA). The **Trevor Story** pick at 30 was tough — Story hit just .238 with 16 HR while adjusting to Boston. **John Means** in Round 12 threw 28.2 innings before his arm exploded. This draft screams "good process, bad luck."
**Grade: C+**

**Canberra Wombats (Pick #3)**
No historical data on the Wombats, but this draft suggests someone who knows what they're doing. **Juan Soto** at 3 is the obvious play — he hit .314 with a .401 OBP before getting traded. **Xander Bogaerts** (.307, 15 HR) and **Wander Franco** (.277, 6 HR in 83 games due to injury) gave solid infield production. **Jose Altuve** in Round 4? The man hit .300 with 28 homers at age 32. That's a steal. **Yu Darvish** (Round 6) threw 194 innings of 3.10 ERA baseball. The weak spots: **Jared Walsh** fell off a cliff (.215, 15 HR), and **Josh Bell** at pick 166 was fine but forgettable. This is a quietly excellent draft with no glaring holes.
**Grade: A-**

**Houston Trashcan Decoders (Pick #4)**
Okay, the team name alone deserves points. **José Ramírez** at 4 was perfect — he hit .280 with 29 HR and 20 steals, classic J-Ram. **Manny Machado** in Round 2 delivered one of his best seasons (.298, 32 HR, 102 RBI). But here's where the Decoders really decoded: **Austin Riley** in Round 5 at pick 60. ROUND FIVE. Riley hit .273 with 38 home runs and finished 7th in MVP voting. That's an absolute heist. **Pablo López** in Round 10 (3.75 ERA, solid K numbers) and **Logan Gilbert** in Round 12 (3.20 ERA, 185 Ks) showed excellent pitching instincts. The misses? **Tyler O'Neill** imploded (.228, 14 HR), and **Nick Castellanos** signed a big deal then promptly hit .263 with 13 HR. But the Riley pick alone makes this draft a winner.
**Grade: A**

**HVR Hits Different (Pick #5)**
Coming off an 8th-place finish, HVR needed to hit differently. They... did not. **Bo Bichette** at 5 was fine (.290, 24 HR), but **Shane Bieber** in Round 2 threw just 118 innings with diminished stuff. **Whit Merrifield** in Round 3 was brutal — .240 before getting traded, completely lost his ability to steal bases (7 total). **José Abreu** was solid (.304, 15 HR), but **Charlie Morton** (Round 5) gave them a 4.34 ERA and the look of a man who's been pitching professionally since the Eisenhower administration. **Tommy Edman** (Round 8, pick 108) was actually excellent — .265 with 32 steals and elite positional flexibility. But **Trevor Rogers** in Round 7 went from Rookie of the Year candidate to 5.47 ERA cautionary tale. The **Adolis García** pick at 192 was a late-round gem (27 HR), but this draft has too many misses in the middle rounds.
**Grade: C**

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Pick #6)**
**Mike Trout** at 6 is always the right call — when he plays. Trout gave you .283 with 40 HR in 119 games, which is incredible on a rate basis and frustrating on a counting stats basis. **Julio Urías** in Round 2 (2.16 ERA, 17 wins) was tremendous value. But then things get weird: **Salvador Perez** in Round 3 (pick 34) hit .254 with 23 HR, a significant regression from his monster 2021. **Brandon Lowe** hit .221 and spent half the season hurt. **Joey Votto** in Round 8 was 38 years old and hit .205. **Adam Wainwright** in Round 11 was fine (3.71 ERA, 191 IP), but this roster has a "I drafted players I liked watching in 2017" energy that didn't translate to 2022.
**Grade: C+**

**Will Smith's Right Hand (Pick #7)**
This team name aged beautifully (or terribly, depending on your perspective on the Oscars). **Bryce Harper** at 7 was the correct pick — he hit .286 with 18 HR before a thumb injury ended his season in June. Then comes **Aaron Judge** at pick 22. PICK TWENTY-TWO. He hit 62 home runs. Sixty-two! This is like finding a Picasso at a garage sale. The draft continues with **Josh Hader** in Round 3 (elite until the Padres trade, then shaky), and **Shane McClanahan** in Round 8 (pick 106!) — McClanahan threw 166 innings with a 2.54 ERA and finished 5th in Cy Young voting. That's two of the best picks in this entire draft. The downside: **Max Muncy** hit .196, **Anthony Rendon** played 47 games, and **Aroldis Chapman** became unusable. But Judge and McClanahan are draft-defining picks.
**Grade: A+**

**Albuquerque Bombers (Pick #8)**
Finished 2nd in 2021, so expectations were high. **Freddie Freeman** at 8 delivered (.325, 21 HR), and **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 2 was sensational (.306, 37 HR, 97 RBI). **Sandy Alcantara** in Round 3 won the Cy Young with a 2.28 ERA and 228 innings. That's a first-round ace at pick 36. The Bombers also grabbed **Bobby Witt Jr.** in Round 6 — the rookie hit .254 with 20 HR and 30 steals, legitimate five-category contributor. But there are landmines everywhere: **Robbie Ray** (Round 4) went from Cy Young to 3.71 ERA with diminished control. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at pick 120 in Round 9 looked like a genius stash... until he got suspended for PEDs and played zero games. **Eloy Jiménez** hit .295 but played just 84 games. This draft is talented but cursed.
**Grade: B+**

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Pick #9)**
**Mookie Betts** at 9 is always safe — .269, 35 HR, elite defense (not that defense matters in fantasy, but you know what I mean). But **Walker Buehler** in Round 2 was a catastrophe: 65 innings, 4.02 ERA, then season-ending elbow surgery. **Marcus Semien** came back to earth (.248, 26 HR) after his monster 2021, and **Kris Bryant** was a black hole (.306 but only 42 games before injury). **Ian Anderson** in Round 9 had a 5.00 ERA. **Austin Meadows** in Round 12 played 36 games. This draft is a graveyard of injured players and regression candidates. The saving grace: **Will Smith** (catcher) in Round 6 hit .260 with 24 HR, and **Nolan Arenado** (Round 5) delivered 30 HR and 103 RBI. But the Buehler pick haunts everything.
**Grade: C-**

**happymomof4@Y!.com (Pick #10)**
First of all, incredible email address energy. **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at 10 is fascinating — someone else took Ohtani (Pitcher) later, so this league split him. Ohtani hit .273 with 34 HR as a hitter alone, plus the steals. **Ozzie Albies** in Round 2 hit .247 with 8 HR before a broken foot ended his season in June. **Dylan Cease** in Round 6 (2.20 ERA, 227 Ks) was one of the best pitching values in this entire draft. **Rhys Hoskins** (Round 11) hit 30 HR. But **Freddy Peralta** (Round 4) threw just 78 innings, **Adalberto Mondesi** played 15 games (classic Mondesi), and **Franmil Reyes** hit .213 before getting released. This draft has massive peaks and valleys.
**Grade: B-**

**Benchwarmers (Pick #11)**
Finished 5th in 2021, and this draft screams "I'm going to do it again." **Corbin Burnes** at 11 is an ace (2.94 ERA, 243 Ks), and **Brandon Woodruff** in Round 2 was similarly excellent (3.05 ERA) when healthy. Going pitcher-pitcher to start is a strategy, I suppose. **Randy Arozarena** in Round 4 (.263, 20 HR, 32 SB) was exactly what he was supposed to be. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 7 hit 46 home runs — FORTY-SIX — at pick 95. That's absurd value. **Daulton Varsho** (Round 9) emerged as a star (.235, 27 HR, catcher eligibility). The concern: **Javier Báez** hit .238 with strikeouts galore, **Christian Yelich** continues to not be 2018 Yelich (.252, 14 HR), and **Liam Hendriks** in Round 3 feels like a reach for a closer. But Schwarber and Varsho make up for a lot.
**Grade: B+**

**Hiroshima Radiation (Pick #12)**
**Max Scherzer** at 12 is bold — he's 37, he's expensive, he's Max Scherzer (2.29 ERA in 145 innings). **Luis Robert Jr.** in Round 2 is tantalizing (.284, 12 HR in 98 games) but injury-prone. **Byron Buxton** in Round 3 is also tantalizing (.224, 28 HR in 92 games) and also injury-prone. This is the "I love ceiling, I hate floors" draft. **Paul Goldschmidt** in Round 4 (pick 45) won the MVP — .317, 35 HR, 115 RBI. That's the pick of the draft. **J.T. Realmuto** (Round 6) gave elite catcher production (.276, 22 HR). **Joe Ryan** in Round 14 (3.55 ERA, solid K rate) was a nice late find. The problem? Too much injury risk concentrated at the top. When Robert and Buxton play, this roster is scary. When they don't, you're scrambling.
**Grade: B**

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Pick #13)**
*Bob's Burgers* reference in the team name — I see you. **Gerrit Cole** at 13 is fine (3.50 ERA, 257 Ks), though taking a pitcher this early always feels like bringing a fork to a soup competition. **Kyle Tucker** in Round 2 (.257, 30 HR, 25 SB) is a five-category monster. **Francisco Lindor** bounced back (.270, 26 HR), and **Pete Alonso** (Round 4) hit 40 HR. But wait — **Shohei Ohtani (Pitcher)** in Round 6! If you're the team that also has Ohtani the hitter... you win the league. You don't? You get 2.33 ERA and 166 Ks, which is still excellent but not unfair. **Luis Castillo** in Round 10 was phenomenal post-trade (2.86 combined ERA). This is a well-constructed roster with starting pitching depth.
**Grade: B+**

**General Hospital (Pick #14)**
The snake-turn bonus gave them **Ronald Acuña Jr.** AND **Rafael Devers** with picks 14-15. Acuña returned from the ACL to hit .266 with 15 HR in 119 games — not vintage Acuña, but promising. Devers hit .295 with 27 HR, rock solid. **Justin Verlander** in Round 6 won the Cy Young at age 39 (1.75 ERA, are you kidding me). **Corey Seager** (Round 7) hit .245 with 33 HR. **Zac Gallen** in Round 11 emerged as an ace (2.54 ERA). But **Aaron Nola** (Round 3) had an uneven year (3.25 ERA but frustrating inconsistency), and **Joey Gallo** in Round 12 hit .160. Point-one-six-zero. He might be the worst pick in this entire draft relative to round. Still, the Verlander pick alone deserves respect.
**Grade: B+**

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The Three Best Value Picks:**

1. **Aaron Judge, Round 2, Pick 22 (Will Smith's Right Hand)** — He hit 62 home runs. He won the MVP. He fell to pick 22 because everyone convinced themselves the short porch wouldn't last. Wrong.

2. **Austin Riley, Round 5, Pick 60 (Houston Trashcan Decoders)** — 38 home runs and 7th in MVP voting... in the FIFTH ROUND. This is why you draft breakout candidates aggressively.

3. **Shane McClanahan, Round 8, Pick 106 (Will Smith's Right Hand)** — Cy Young finalist, 2.54 ERA, and he was pick 106. Will Smith's Right Hand had a historic draft.

**The Three Worst Picks:**

1. **Joey Gallo, Round 12, Pick 155 (General Hospital)** — .160 average. He was cut by the Yankees. He was unplayable. Even in Round 12, roster spots matter.

2. **Trevor Rogers, Round 7, Pick 89 (HVR Hits Different)** — The Rookie of the Year narrative fooled everyone. A 5.47 ERA in 114 innings? That's replacement level.

3. **Nelson Cruz, Round 10, Pick 140 (BaseballPro77)** — He was 41 years old. The power was gone (.234, 10 HR). This is a "remember when" pick in a league where you can't afford nostalgia.

**V. The Verdict: How the Draft Shaped the 2022 Season**

**Will Smith's Right Hand** deserves to be studied in fantasy laboratories. Getting **Aaron Judge** at 22 and **Shane McClanahan** at 106 is the fantasy equivalent of hitting blackjack twice in a row. Those two picks alone probably won them multiple weeks. Even with Harper's injury (which cost them their first-round pick for half the season), the Judge selection made this a championship-caliber roster. If they finished outside the top two, something went horribly wrong with their waiver wire management.

**Houston Trashcan Decoders** built a sneaky powerhouse. **José Ramírez** leading the way, **Austin Riley** breaking out, **Pablo López** and **Logan Gilbert** providing pitching depth — this is how you draft when you don't have a top-three pick. Their 5th-round Riley selection will be remembered as one of the smartest picks in league history. The only question is whether they made enough moves during the season to capitalize.

The biggest draft-related tragedies? **Sugar Land Skeeters** bet heavily on **Walker Buehler** and **Kris Bryant**, both of whom essentially disappeared due to injury. When your Round 2 and Round 4 picks combine for 107 games, you're fighting with one arm behind your back. **Zach's Team** similarly suffered from the **Lucas Giolito** implosion (Round 2, 4.90 ERA) and various injury issues.

**Canberra Wombats** quietly assembled a roster that probably contended all year. **Juan Soto** (elite), **Jose Altuve** (Round 4 steal), **Yu Darvish** (consistent) — this is a draft with no major wins but also no catastrophic losses. Sometimes the best drafts are the ones without memorable disasters.

The **Paul Goldschmidt** selection at pick 45 (Hiroshima Radiation) deserves special mention. Taking a 34-year-old first baseman in Round 4 felt safe and uninspired... until he won the MVP. Fantasy baseball rewards patience and veterans more than we want to admit.

**VI. The 2022 Draft: Final Grades & Legacy**

**Overall Draft Class Grade: B**

This was a solid, unspectacular draft class with a few transcendent picks and a handful of inexcusable reaches. The injury bug bit hard — Buehler, Bryant, Harper, Albies, Acuña (limited), Tatis (suspended) — which made depth and late-round value more important than ever.

**Best Drafting Decision in Retrospect:** Will Smith's Right Hand selecting Aaron Judge at 22. This pick will be referenced in league lore forever. "Remember when Judge hit 62 and someone got him in Round 2?"

**Worst Drafting Decision in Retrospect:** Anyone who aggressively targeted pitching injury risks in the early rounds (looking at you, Buehler at pick 20) learned a painful lesson about the fragility of arms.

The 2022 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball draft will be remembered for one thing above all else: the year someone slept on Aaron Judge, and the entire league paid the price. In fantasy baseball, as in life, the biggest mistakes aren't the players you pick — they're the players you pass on. Sixty-two home runs. At pick 22. We'll never let them forget it.
